draft.introduction: Ūji (“Being-Time”) is Dōgen’s radical philosophy of temporality. Time does not flow from past to future; each moment is the complete expression of all existence. Being is time; time is being.

translation.text: An ancient buddha said: For the time being, I stand on the highest mountain peak. For the time being, I walk on the bottom of the deepest ocean. The “time being” means that time, just as it is, is being, and being is all time.
translation.original_text: 有時は高峰の頂に立ち、有時は深海の底に行く

translation.text: Do not think of time as merely flying by. If time were simply flying away, there would be gaps between you and time. Mountains are time; oceans are time. Without time, no mountain could appear.
translation.original_text: 光陰の去来とのみ学すべからず

translator_notes.note: Dōgen’s philosophy of time anticipates Heidegger’s “Being and Time” by seven centuries. Both reject the notion of time as a container through which things pass. For Dōgen, each moment is not in time; each moment is time.
